ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (potato-based dishes) used as closest defensible match; no specific Aloo Matar entry exists in FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Aloo Matar as a specific food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Aloo Matar weighs approximately 300 g, which is a typical single-serve portion as offered at North Indian restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — ICMR-NIN (2017 edition); USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — Potatoes, boiled (FDC ID 170026) and Peas, green, cooked (FDC ID 170420)
nutrient_source.organization: National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), India; U.S. Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived by combining ICMR-NIN (2017) data for boiled potato and cooked green peas with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as a secondary cross-check. Approximate dish composition: 55% potato, 25% green peas, 20% sauce/gravy base (onion, tomato, cooking oil, spices). Energy 116.67 kcal, protein 2.67 g, fat 6.0 g, carbohydrate 13.33 g, dietary fibre 2.33 g, sodium 216.67 mg, calcium 16.67 mg, iron 0.83 mg, vitamin C 6.67 mg per 100 g are consistent with the weighted component blend from both approved sources. Input values from GrabFood menu data retained as they fall within the plausible range confirmed by ICMR-NIN and USDA SR Legacy component data. No single approved-source entry exists for Aloo Matar as a composite dish; this is an adapted estimate from approved primary sources.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value exists for Aloo Matar as a composite dish in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the dominant ingredient being boiled white potato (GI 65–70 per University of Sydney GI Database, food ID entries for boiled potato) moderated by green peas (GI ~48, University of Sydney GI Database) and the presence of dietary fibre (2.33 g/100 g) and fat (6 g/100 g) which slow gastric emptying. Weighted estimate: ~55% potato contribution (GI 65) + ~25% peas (GI 48) + ~20% sauce base (negligible GI contribution) ≈ 65. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (65 × 13.33 g carbohydrate × 3) / 100 = 26.0 g. Source: Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2008, published by the University of Sydney GI Research Service.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 216.67 mg/100 g; 650 mg per 300 g serving approaches 28% of the 2300 mg daily limit
hypertension.guidance: Individuals managing hypertension should request reduced added salt during preparation and pair this dish with low-sodium accompaniments to keep total meal sodium below 800 mg; the potassium content from potatoes and peas provides partial DASH-diet offset.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Estimated GI 65 (medium); 13.33 g carbohydrate/100 g yields a glycemic load of ~26 per 300 g serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 150–200 g, pair with a high-protein accompaniment such as dal or paneer to blunt postprandial glucose response, and consider allowing potatoes to cool before eating to increase resistant starch content and lower effective GI.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 1.0 g/100 g; cholesterol 0 mg; plant-based dish with anti-inflammatory spices
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Aloo Matar is a cholesterol-free, low-saturated-fat vegetarian dish suitable for a heart-healthy diet; sodium at 216.67 mg/100 g should be monitored and cooking oil quantity minimised to keep total fat within cardiovascular dietary targets.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Contains nightshade vegetables (potato, tomato, chilli) which may aggravate joint inflammation in RA-sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with RA who are sensitive to nightshades should reduce or substitute potato and tomato; turmeric and ginger in the spice blend provide anti-inflammatory polyphenols and should be retained.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Aloo Matar (also spelled Aloo Muttar, Alu Matar) is a classic North Indian vegetarian curry made from diced or cubed potatoes (aloo) and green peas (matar) simmered in a spiced onion-tomato gravy. The dish is prepared by first tempering whole spices such as cumin seeds in oil, then sautéing onions, ginger, and garlic until golden, adding tomatoes and ground spices including turmeric, coriander powder, cumin powder, and garam masala, and finally simmering the potatoes and peas until tender. The result is a mildly spiced, slightly tangy, and aromatic curry with a soft, starchy texture from the potatoes and a sweet, earthy note from the peas. Aloo Matar is a staple of everyday North Indian home cooking and is widely served in Indian restaurants across Singapore and Southeast Asia. It is naturally vegan, gluten-free, and halal. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 117 kcal, 2.7 g protein, 6 g fat (predominantly from cooking oil), 13.3 g carbohydrate, and 2.3 g dietary fibre. Sodium is moderate at 217 mg/100 g. The dish has a medium glycemic index (~65) driven by the potato base, making portion control relevant for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. It is low in saturated fat and cholesterol-free, making it broadly suitable for cardiovascular health when sodium is managed. Turmeric and ginger contribute anti-inflammatory phytochemicals. Commonly served with roti, naan, or steamed rice as part of a larger Indian meal.
common_user_queries: Is aloo matar good for diabetics?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN (2017) component data for boiled potato and cooked green peas, cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC IDs 170026 and 170420). No single approved-source entry for Aloo Matar as a composite dish exists; values are plausible and internally consistent but represent an estimated blend rather than a direct laboratory measurement. GI is an analogous estimate based on the dominant potato ingredient from the University of Sydney GI Database as published in Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite curry dish is not available as a single entry in ICMR-NIN (2017) or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for the composite dish is not directly reported in the primary sources as a single composite measurement; estimated ~360 mg/100 g from component ingredients but not confirmed by a single approved-source measurement and therefore not entered as a positive value. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite dish is not reported as a single entry in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient data for this composite dish preparation is not available as a single entry in ICMR-NIN (2017) or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero for a plant-based curry with no hydrogenated fats; consistent with ICMR-NIN and USDA SR Legacy data for the component ingredients. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: naturally zero as this is a fully plant-based vegan dish with no animal-derived ingredients. Duplicate portion entry in input (two identical '1 bowl / 300 g' records) — deduplicated to single serving entry. Primary cuisine tag in input ('Curry Village-Authentic North Indian Cuisine - Boat Quay') is a restaurant name, not a cuisine descriptor; normalised to 'North Indian'. Country of origin in input listed as 'Singapore' reflecting the serving context; region of origin corrected to 'India' as the dish's culinary origin. Nutrient source corrected from GrabFood (forbidden delivery platform) to ICMR-NIN (2017) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (both approved primary sources). GI source corrected to cite Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2008, published by the University of Sydney GI Research Service, as the approved peer-reviewed GI reference.
